petitioner_background: The petitioner is a self-represented Chinese national seeking classification as an alien of extraordinary ability under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act as a sculptor, decorating expert, and designer working in glass art. He has been residing in the United States since 2000, and filed the instant petition on March 28, 2005. His claimed credentials include awards from Jilin Province and other Chinese entities, alleged memberships in Chinese art associations, and purported publications and exhibitions—many of which the AAO determined to be fraudulent submissions misrepresenting the work of Dale Chihuly, Sunny Wang, and Rita Reif as his own.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted ten award certificates: (1) a certificate for second place at the first annual art festival in Jilin Province (October 1989); (2) a certificate from the Public Affair Department of Jilin Province naming him 'one of the Talented Young Artists' (December 1997); (3) a certificate of encouragement from the Jilin Government for his work 'Chinese Dragon' at the 11th Annual Culture Art Exhibition (December 1997); (4) a certificate from the News Publishing Department of the People's Republic of China for 'best display award' for his work 'Joy' at a new millennium photography competition (January 2000); (5) a certificate from the Contemporary Art Association of the People's Republic of China for his glasswork 'Kindness' winning 'outstanding award' at an unidentified exhibition (June 2000); (6) a certificate for his glasswork 'Scenery' winning first place in the Millennium Luxury Architect Design Competition (January 2000); (7) a certificate for an 'outstanding award' at the 4th Art Festival in Jilin Province (October 1992); (8) a certificate for the 'Outstanding Achievement Award' from the Jilin Government (December 1997); (9) an 'Honor Certificate' from the Jilin Government naming him 'one of the top ten young artists in the nation' (August 1998); and (10) an 'Honor Certificate' for his glass sculpture skill winning first place in the 4th national exhibit competition (August 2000). He also submitted a document about a Chinese modern glass art exhibition in Beijing sponsored by the Chinese construction glass and industry glass association.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The translations accompanying the award certificates were not certified as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3). Items 1, 2, 3, 7, 8, and 9 reflect only provincial recognition rather than national or international recognition. Items 2 and 9 are 'young artist' awards that offer no meaningful comparison between the petitioner and the most experienced and practiced sculptors, with no indication he faced competition from his entire field rather than his age group, and thus are not an indication he has reached the 'very top of the field of endeavor.' The Beijing competition document contained a date discrepancy (June 2000 vs. April 2004) that the petitioner failed to resolve with competent objective evidence per Matter of Ho. For all ten items, there was no evidence of contemporaneous publicity surrounding the awards or evidence showing they command a substantial level of recognition, and no supporting documentation from the awarding entities or print media to establish they are nationally or internationally recognized. Additionally, the record included no evidence of awards subsequent to 2000, indicating the petitioner's acclaim has not been sustained.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted certificates reflecting his appointment as a board member of the Chinese Art Association (August 2000) and the Art Association of Jilin Province (September 1997). In response to the director's request for evidence, he also submitted a document entitled 'Chinese artist association' citing that organization's 'draft' regulations.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: There was no evidence showing the duration of the petitioner's appointments or whether he remains an active member of these organizations. The record did not include the membership bylaws or official admission requirements for the Art Association of Jilin Province. The 'Chinese artist association' document could not be accepted because its English translation was not certified as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3). The evidence failed to show that admission required outstanding achievement or that he was evaluated by national or international experts in consideration of his admission.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted what he alleged was an article appearing in the September 29, 1990 issue of People Daily News, and what he alleged was a 2001 article in American Art Glass Quarterly entitled 'Interview with [petitioner].'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO determined both submissions were fraudulent. The alleged American Art Glass Quarterly article was actually 'Interview with Dale Chihuly' from Volume 3, No. 1, 1985, into which the petitioner falsely substituted his name. The alleged September 29, 1990 People Daily News article was actually written by Rita Reif and appeared in a September 29, 1971 issue of The New York Times about Dale Chihuly. The AAO issued a June 1, 2006 notice giving the petitioner an opportunity to rebut these findings, but he failed to respond. There was no evidence the petitioner was the primary subject of published material in major media.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted what he alleged was an article he wrote appearing in Contemporary Glass Artists and Their Work (1995); what he alleged was an article he wrote entitled '[The petitioner] Glass 2004' from an unidentified publication; and what he alleged was evidence of his authorship of a book entitled The Spectacle of Beauty of the Glass Work, including a book cover and description.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO determined all of these submissions were fraudulent. The 1995 Contemporary Glass Artists and Their Work article was originally written by Dale Chihuly (obtained from chihuly.com). The '[Petitioner] Glass 2004' article included material originally written by Sunny Wang and was originally entitled 'Sunny Wang Glass 2004' (obtained from koru-hk.com). The book description for The Spectacle of Beauty of the Glass Work related to Dale Chihuly rather than the petitioner (obtained from chihuly.com and portlandpress.chihovvenpar.html). The petitioner failed to respond to the AAO's June 1, 2006 notice providing an opportunity to rebut these findings.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted what he alleged were images and descriptions of his exhibited artwork, and promotional material dated October 7, 2004 for an alleged exhibition of his work entitled '[Petitioner] 2004, Houston, TX' at the Contemporary Arts Museum/Modern Art Gallery of Houston, Texas.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO determined these submissions were fraudulent. The promotional material was actually for an exhibition of Sunny Wang's work in Hong Kong rather than the petitioner's work in Houston (original obtained from koru-hk.com). The images and descriptions of alleged exhibited artwork were actually of Dale Chihuly's work (original obtained from okcmoa.com). The record included no information about the Houston museum or evidence from an official representative confirming its display of the petitioner's work. The petitioner failed to respond to the AAO's June 1, 2006 notice. Specific names, dates, and locations of any other exhibitions were not identified, and no contemporaneous evidence in the form of event programs or art brochures was submitted. There was no evidence the petitioner's works have been displayed at significant national venues, featured alongside artists with national or international reputations, or regularly shown at exclusive venues devoted to his work.

outcome_reasoning: The appeal was dismissed with a finding of fraud. The AAO found that the petitioner knowingly submitted fraudulent documents misrepresenting the artistic accomplishments of Dale Chihuly, Sunny Wang, and Rita Reif as his own across multiple criteria, and failed to respond to the AAO's June 1, 2006 derogatory information notice providing an opportunity to rebut these findings. Under section 212(a)(6)(C) of the Act, the petitioner sought to procure an immigration benefit through fraud and willful misrepresentation of material facts. The petitioner also failed to demonstrate he met at least three of the criteria at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3), failed to show sustained national or international acclaim, and failed to provide clear evidence under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(5) that he was coming to the United States to continue work in his area of expertise. The finding of fraud shall be considered in any future proceeding where admissibility is an issue.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act
Spencer Enterprises, Inc. v. United States, 229 F. Supp. 2d 1025 (E.D. Cal. 2001), aff'd, 345 F.3d 683 (9th Cir. 2003)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The AAO made an explicit finding of fraud under section 212(a)(6)(C) of the Act, determining that the petitioner knowingly submitted fraudulent documents to mislead CIS and the AAO. Specifically, the petitioner: (1) falsely substituted his name into an 'Interview with Dale Chihuly' article from American Art Glass Quarterly (1985); (2) plagiarized a Rita Reif article from The New York Times (September 29, 1971) about Dale Chihuly and passed it off as a September 29, 1990 People Daily News article he wrote; (3) plagiarized a Dale Chihuly article and claimed authorship in Contemporary Glass Artists and Their Work (1995); (4) plagiarized Sunny Wang's work for an article titled '[Petitioner] Glass 2004'; (5) misrepresented a Dale Chihuly book description as relating to his own claimed book The Spectacle of Beauty of the Glass Work; (6) misrepresented promotional material for a Sunny Wang exhibition in Hong Kong as an exhibition of his own work in Houston; and (7) misrepresented images of Dale Chihuly's artwork as his own. The AAO issued a derogatory information notice on June 1, 2006 under 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(16)(i), but the petitioner failed to respond within the 30-day period. Additional procedural issues included: uncertified foreign language translations violating 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3); the petitioner's failure to submit a promised appellate brief within 30 days (more than eight months had elapsed); and unresolved date discrepancies in submitted documents per Matter of Ho. The finding of fraud shall be considered in any future proceeding where admissibility is an issue.
